questionText: The reading of the ammeter $(A)$ in steady state in the following circuit (assuming negligible internal resistance of the ammeter) is $\_\_\_\_$ A.
subject: Physics
solution:
In a DC circuit, a capacitor reaches a steady state after being connected for a long time. In this state the capacitor is fully charged and it acts as an infinite resistance (an open circuit). The means no current flows through the branch containing the $10 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ capacitor and the $8 \Omega$ resistor connected in series with it.

The two $8 \Omega$ resistors $\mathrm{R}_4$ and $\mathrm{R}_5$ are in parallel.

$$ \mathrm{R}_{4,5}=\frac{8 \times 8}{8+8}=\frac{64}{16}=4 \Omega $$

This $\mathrm{R}_{4,5}=4 \Omega$ equivalent is in series with the $\mathrm{R}_3=4 \Omega$ resistor.

$$ \mathrm{R}_{3,4,5}=4 \Omega+4 \Omega=8 \Omega $$

This $\mathrm{R}_{3,4,5}=8 \Omega$ combination is now in parallel with the $\mathrm{R}_2=8 \Omega$ vertical resistor in the middle.

$$ \mathrm{R}_{2,3,4,5}=\frac{8 \times 8}{8+8}=4 \Omega $$

Finally, this $\mathrm{R}_{2,3,4,5}=4 \Omega$ is in series with the $\mathrm{R}_1=1 \Omega$.

$$ \mathrm{R}_{\text {total }}=1 \Omega+4 \Omega=5 \Omega $$

Using Ohm's Law for the whole circuit:

$$ \mathrm{i}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\text {total }}}=\frac{10}{5} \mathrm{~A}=2 \mathrm{~A}=\mathrm{i}_1 $$

The reading of the ammeter is equal to the value of $\mathrm{i}_3$.

Since the resistances $\mathrm{R}_2$ and $\mathrm{R}_{3,4,5}$ are equal to $8 \Omega$, the current splits equally, $\mathrm{i}_2=\mathrm{i}_3$

Using Kirchhoff's current law at junction, $\mathrm{i}_1=\mathrm{i}_2+\mathrm{i}_3=2 \mathrm{i}_2=2 \mathrm{i}_3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{i}_2=\mathrm{i}_3=\frac{\mathrm{i}_1}{2}$

So, the reading of the ammeter is $\mathrm{i}_3=\frac{\mathrm{i}_1}{2}=\frac{\mathrm{i}}{2}=1 \mathrm{~A}$

Therefore, the ammeter reading is 1A in steady state. Hence, the correct option is (B).

questionText: The voltage and the current between $A$ and $B$ points shown in the circuit are $\_\_\_\_$ .
subject: Physics
solution:
The circuit consists of four parallel branches between points A and B :

Branch 1: $\mathrm{E}_1=27 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{r}_1=3 \Omega$

Branch 2: $\mathrm{E}_2=27 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{r}_2=3 \Omega$

Branch 3: Two cells in series ( 14 V and 13 V ) and a resistor. The total EMF is $\mathrm{E}_3=14+13=27 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{r}_3= 3 \Omega$

Branch 4: $\mathrm{E}_4=27 \mathrm{~V}, \mathrm{r}_4=3 \Omega$

Since all branches have the same EMF ( 27 V ) and are connected in parallel with the same polarity, the equivalent EMF of the combination is

$$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{E}_1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{\mathrm{E}_2}{\mathrm{r}_2}+\frac{\mathrm{E}_3}{\mathrm{r}_3}+\frac{\mathrm{E}_4}{\mathrm{r}_4}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_3}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_4}}=\frac{\frac{27}{3}+\frac{27}{3}+\frac{27}{3}+\frac{27}{3}}{\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}}=27 \mathrm{~V} $$

The four $3 \Omega$ resistors are in parallel. The equivalent internal resistance is :

$$ \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}=\frac{4}{3} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{3}{4} \Omega=0.75 \Omega $$

So, the equivalent circuit is,

Between points A and B , the load consisting of two $3 \Omega$ resistors in series.

So, total external resistance $\mathrm{R}=3+3=6 \Omega$.

The total current (i) in the circuit is :

$$ i=\frac{E_{e q}}{R+r_{e q}}=\frac{27}{6+0.75}=\frac{27}{6.75}=4 \mathrm{~A} $$

The terminal voltage ( $\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{AB}}$ ) across points A and B is :

$$ V_{A B}=E_{e q}-\mathrm{ir}_{\mathrm{eq}} \text { or } V_{A B}=\mathrm{I} \times \mathrm{R} $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ V_{A B}=4 \times 6=24 \mathrm{~V} $$

Therefore, the voltage is 24 V and the current is 4 A .

Thus, the correct option is (B).

questionText:
Refer to the figure given below, current between terminals $A$ and $B$ is

$\_\_\_\_$ A.
subject: Physics
solution:
We have three identical branches in parallel. Each branch has an emf $\mathrm{E}=5 \mathrm{~V}$ and an internal resistance $\mathrm{r}= 3 \Omega$.

When cells are connected in series with their polarity in order, the equivalent emf is given as,

$$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{eq}(\mathrm{~s})}=\mathrm{E}_1+\mathrm{E}_2+\cdots+\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{n}} $$

The equivalent internal resistance is given as $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{eq}(\mathrm{s})}=\mathrm{r}_1+\mathrm{r}_2+\cdots+\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}$.

When cells are connected in parallel with their polarity in order, the equivalent emf is given as

$$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{eq}(\mathrm{p})}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{E}_1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{\mathrm{E}_2}{\mathrm{r}_2}+\cdots+\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{n}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_1}+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{n}}}} $$

The equivalent internal resistance is given as $\frac{1}{r_{e q(p)}}=\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}+\cdots+\frac{1}{r_n}$.

For each branch, total emf is, $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{eq}}=(5+5+5+) \mathrm{V}=15 \mathrm{~V}$

Total internal resistance is, $\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{eq}}=(3+3+3) \Omega=9 \Omega$

So, the circuit simplifies to;

Now all three branches containing cell are in parallel.

Using the parallel combination of cells;

$$ \mathrm{E}_{\text {total }}=\frac{\frac{15}{9}+\frac{15}{9}+\frac{15}{9}}{\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{9}}=\frac{45}{3}=15 \mathrm{~V} $$

The total internal resistance is,

$$ \frac{1}{\mathrm{r}_{\text {total }}}=\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{9}+\frac{1}{9}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}_{\text {total }}=3 \Omega $$

And the total external resistance between A and B is $\mathrm{R}_{\text {total }}=(3+3+3) \Omega=9 \Omega$

So, the circuit simplifies to,

Using Ohm's Law $\left(I=\frac{V}{R}\right)$ for the entire circuit, the current through $A$ and $B$ will be

$i=\frac{E_{\text {total }}}{R_{\text {net }}}=\frac{15}{(3+9)}=\frac{15}{12} A=$ 1.25 A

Thus, the correct option is (B).

questionText: Refer to the figure given below. The values of $I_1, I_2$ and $I_3$ are $\_\_\_\_$ .
subject: Physics
solution:
Using Kirchhoff's current law, the net accumulation of current at a junction is zero.

So, at junction $\mathrm{A}, \mathrm{I}_1-\mathrm{I}_3-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{AD}}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{AD}}=\mathrm{I}_1-\mathrm{I}_3$

And at junction $\mathrm{D}, \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{AD}}+\mathrm{I}_2-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{DB}}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_1-\mathrm{I}_3+\mathrm{I}_2-\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{DB}}=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{DB}}=\mathrm{I}_1-\mathrm{I}_3+\mathrm{I}_2$

Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law in closed loop I,

$$ -4\left(\mathrm{I}_1-\mathrm{I}_3\right)-2\left(\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2-\mathrm{I}_3\right)-\mathrm{I}_1+10=0 $$

$$ -7 \mathrm{I}_1-2 \mathrm{I}_2+6 \mathrm{I}_3=-10 \ldots (i) $$

In loop (II)

$$ I_1+10-4 I_3-2\left(I_3-I_2\right)=0 $$

$$ 2 \mathrm{I}_2-6 \mathrm{I}_3-\mathrm{I}_1=-10 \ldots \text { (ii) } $$

In loop III,

$$ -5-2\left(\mathrm{I}_3-\mathrm{I}_2\right)+2\left(\mathrm{I}_1+\mathrm{I}_2-\mathrm{I}_3\right)=0 $$

$$ 2 \mathrm{I}_1+4 \mathrm{I}_2-4 \mathrm{I}_3=5 .....(iii) $$

From equation (iii),

$$ I_1=\frac{5-4 I_2+4 I_3}{2} \ldots (iv) $$

On substituting equation (iv) in equation (ii);

$$ 2 \mathrm{I}_2-6 \mathrm{I}_3-\frac{5-4 \mathrm{I}_2+4 \mathrm{I}_3}{2}=-10 $$

$\Rightarrow 4 \mathrm{I}_2-12 \mathrm{I}_3-5+4 \mathrm{I}_2-4 \mathrm{I}_3=-20$

$\Rightarrow 8 \mathrm{I}_2-16 \mathrm{I}_3=-15$

$\Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2-2 \mathrm{I}_3=-\frac{15}{8}$

$$ \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_2=2 \mathrm{I}_3-\frac{15}{8} \ldots(\mathrm{v}) $$

On substituting equation (v) in equation (iv),

$$ \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{5-4\left(2 \mathrm{I}_3-\frac{15}{8}\right)}{2}=\frac{5-8 \mathrm{I}_3+\frac{15}{2}+4 \mathrm{I}_3}{2}=\frac{\frac{25}{2}-4 \mathrm{I}_3}{2}=\frac{25}{4}-2 \mathrm{I}_3 $$

$$\Rightarrow $$ $$ \begin{equation*} I_1=\frac{25}{4}-2 I_3 \ldots (vi) \end{equation*} $$

Substituting equation (v) and (vi) in equation (i);

$$ -7\left(\frac{25}{4}-2 \mathrm{I}_3\right)-2\left(2 \mathrm{I}_3-\frac{15}{8}\right)+6 \mathrm{I}_3=-10 $$

$\begin{gathered}-\frac{175}{4}+14 \mathrm{I}_3-4 \mathrm{I}_3+\frac{15}{4}+6 \mathrm{I}_3=-10 \\ \frac{-175+15}{4}+16 \mathrm{I}_3=-10 \\ \frac{-160}{4}+16 \mathrm{I}_3=-10 \\ -40+16 \mathrm{I}_3=-10 \\ 16 \mathrm{I}_3=30 \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_3=\frac{30}{16} \mathrm{~A} \\ \Rightarrow \mathrm{I}_3=1.875 \mathrm{~A}\end{gathered}$

From equation (v)

$$ I_2=2\left(\frac{15}{8}\right)-\frac{15}{8}=\frac{15}{8}=1.875 \mathrm{~A} $$

From equation (vi),

$$ I_1=\frac{25}{4}-2\left(\frac{15}{8}\right)=\frac{25}{4}-\frac{15}{4}=\frac{10}{4} \mathrm{~A}=2.5 \mathrm{~A} $$

Therefore, the values of currents in the circuit are; $\mathrm{I}_1=2.5 \mathrm{~A}, \mathrm{I}_2=1.875 \mathrm{~A}, \mathrm{I}_3=1.875 \mathrm{~A}$.

Thus, the correct option is (A).

questionText: The net current flowing in the given circuit is __________ A.
subject: Physics
solution: $$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=2 \Omega \\ & \mathrm{I}=\frac{2}{2}=1 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: The reading in the ideal voltmeter $(\mathrm{V})$ shown in the given circuit diagram is :
subject: Physics
solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{i}= & \frac{\mathrm{E}_{\text {eq }}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{8 \times 5}{8 \times 0.2} \\\\ & \mathrm{I}=25 \mathrm{~A} \\\\ & V=\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{ir} \\\\ & =5-0.2 \times 25 \\\\ & =0\end{aligned}$

questionText: Three voltmeters, all having different internal resistances are joined as shown in figure. When some potential difference is applied across $$A$$ and $$B$$, their readings are $$V_1, V_2$$ and $$V_3$$. Choose the correct option.
subject: Physics
solution:
From KVL,

$$\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{1}}+\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{2}}-\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{3}}=\mathbf{0} \Rightarrow \mathrm{V}_1+\mathrm{V}_2=\mathrm{V}_3$$

questionText: In the given circuit, the current in resistance R$$_3$$ is :
subject: Physics
solution:
$$\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}=2 \Omega+2 \Omega+1 \Omega=5 \Omega$$

$$\mathrm{i}=\frac{\mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{eq}}}=\frac{10}{5}=2 \mathrm{~A}$$

Current in resistance

$$\begin{aligned} R_3 & =2 \times\left(\frac{4}{4+4}\right) \\ & =2 \times \frac{4}{8} \\ & =1 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: In the given circuit, the current flowing through the resistance $$20 \Omega$$ is $$0.3 \mathrm{~A}$$, while the ammeter reads $$0.9 \mathrm{~A}$$. The value of $$\mathrm{R}_1$$ is _________ $$\Omega$$.
subject: Physics
solution:
Given, $$\mathrm{i}_1=0.3 \mathrm{~A}, \mathrm{i}_1+\mathrm{i}_2+\mathrm{i}_3=0.9 \mathrm{~A}$$

So, $$\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{AB}}=\mathrm{i}_1 \times 20 \Omega=20 \times 0.3 \mathrm{~V}=6 \mathrm{~V}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{i}_2=\frac{6 \mathrm{~V}}{15 \Omega}=\frac{2}{5} \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{i}_1+\mathrm{i}_2+\mathrm{i}_3=\frac{9}{10} \mathrm{~A} \\ & \frac{3}{10}+\frac{2}{5}+\mathrm{i}_3=\frac{9}{10} \\ & \frac{7}{10}+\mathrm{i}_3=\frac{9}{10} \\ & \mathrm{i}_3=0.2 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \mathrm{So}, \mathrm{i}_3 \times \mathrm{R}_1=6 \mathrm{~V} \\ & (0.2) \mathrm{R}_1=6 \\ & \mathrm{R}_1=\frac{6}{0.2}=30 \Omega \end{aligned}$$

questionText: Two cells are connected in opposition as shown. Cell $$\mathrm{E}_1$$ is of $$8 \mathrm{~V}$$ emf and $$2 \Omega$$ internal resistance; the cell $$\mathrm{E}_2$$ is of $$2 \mathrm{~V}$$ emf and $$4 \Omega$$ internal resistance. The terminal potential difference of cell $$\mathrm{E}_2$$ is __________ V.
subject: Physics
solution:
$$I=\frac{8-2}{2+4}=\frac{6}{6}=1 \mathrm{~A}$$

Applying Kirchhoff from C to B

$$\begin{aligned} & V_C-2-4 \times 1=V_B \\\\ & V_C-V_B=6 \mathrm{~V} \\\\ & =6 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$$

questionText: The current flowing through the $$1 \Omega$$ resistor is $$\frac{n}{10}$$ A. The value of $$n$$ is _______.
subject: Physics
solution:
At $$C$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{v_1}{2}+\frac{v_1-5}{2}+\frac{v_1+10-v_2}{1}=0 \\ & v_2=2 v_1+\frac{15}{2} \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

At $$A$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{v_2-5}{4}+\frac{v_2}{4}+\frac{v_2-10-v_1}{1}=0 \\ & 6 v_2=4 v_1+45 \quad \text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow v_1=0 \\ & \text { and } v_2=\frac{15}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad i=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{~A} \\ & \Rightarrow n=25 \end{aligned}$$

questionText:
In the circuit diagram shown in figure given below, the current flowing through resistance $$3 ~\Omega$$ is $$\frac{x}{3} A$$.

The value of $$x$$ is ___________
subject: Physics
solution:
$$ \mathrm{E}_2-\mathrm{E}_1=8-4=4 \mathrm{~V} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{R} \\\\ & R=2 \Omega \end{aligned} $$




$$ I=\frac{4}{8}=0.5 \mathrm{~A} $$



$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{I}_1=\left(\frac{6}{3+6}\right) \times 0.5 \\\\ & \mathrm{I}_1=\frac{2}{3} \times 0.5=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

$$ I_1=\frac{x}{3}=\frac{1}{3} \therefore x=1 $$

questionText: A network of four resistances is connected to $9 \mathrm{~V}$ battery, as shown in figure. The magnitude of voltage difference between the points $\mathrm{A}$ and $\mathrm{B}$ is __________ $V$.
subject: Physics
solution:
In the circuit $I=\frac{9}{3}=3 \mathrm{~A}$

$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}=2 \times 1.5=3~~......(I) $$

$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{C}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=4 \times 1.5=6~~......(II) $$

$\mathrm{Eq}^{\mathrm{n}}(\mathrm{II})-\mathrm{Eq}^{\mathrm{n}}(\mathrm{I})$

$$ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{A}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{B}}=6-3=3 \text { Volt } $$

questionText: In the given circuit, the value of $$\left| {{{{\mathrm{I_1}} + {\mathrm{I_3}}} \over {{\mathrm{I_2}}}}} \right|$$ is _____________

questionText: Figure shows a part of an electric circuit. The potentials at points $$a, b$$ and $$c$$ are $$30 \mathrm{~V}, 12 \mathrm{~V}$$ and $$2 \mathrm{~V}$$ respectively. The current through the $$20 ~\Omega$$ resistor will be,
subject: Physics

questionText:
The figure shows a circuit having eight resistances of $1 \Omega$ each, labelled $R_{1}$ to $R_{8}$, and two ideal batteries with voltages $\varepsilon_{1}=12 \mathrm{~V}$ and $\varepsilon_{2}=6 \mathrm{~V}$.

Which of the following statement(s) is(are) correct?
options: The magnitude of current flowing through $R_{1}$ is $7.2 \mathrm{~A}$.
The magnitude of current flowing through $R_{2}$ is $1.2 \mathrm{~A}$.
The magnitude of current flowing through $R_{3}$ is $4.8 \mathrm{~A}$.
The magnitude of current flowing through $R_{5}$ is $2.4 \mathrm{~A}$.
subject: Physics
solution:
Point A and B are at same potential so they can be merged/folded.

For loop 1


$$ \begin{aligned} & -\frac{1}{2} x-\frac{1}{2}(x+y)-x+6=0 \\\\ &\Rightarrow -2 x-\frac{y}{2}=-6 \\\\ &\Rightarrow -4 x-y=-12 \\\\ &\Rightarrow 4 x+y=12 .......(1) \end{aligned} $$

For loop 2

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} y+1 y+12+\frac{1}{2}(x+y)=0 \\\\ &\Rightarrow \frac{3}{2} y+\frac{y}{2}+\frac{x}{2}=-12 \\\\ &\Rightarrow 2 y+\frac{x}{2}=-12 \\\\ & \begin{array}{l} \Rightarrow4 y+x=-24 \\\\ \Rightarrow4 y+x-16 x-4 y =-24-48 \end{array} \\\\ &\Rightarrow -15 x=-72 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $x=\frac{72}{15}$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $4\left(\frac{72}{15}\right)+y=12$

$$ \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow y & =12-\frac{288}{15} \\\\ & =\frac{180-288}{15} \\\\ & =\frac{-108}{15}=-7.2 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

Current in $R_1=7.2 \mathrm{~A}$

Current in $R_2=\frac{x+y}{2}=\left(\frac{72}{15}-\frac{108}{15}\right) \frac{1}{2}$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{36}{15}\right)=\frac{2.4}{2} \mathrm{~A} \\\\ & =1.2 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

Current in $R_3=x=4.8 \mathrm{~A}$

Current in $R_5=\frac{1}{2} x=2.4 \mathrm{~A}$

questionText: A current of 15 mA flows in the circuit as shown in figure. The value of potential difference between the points A and B will be:
subject: Physics
solution:
Effective $$R = \left[ {5 + {{5 \times 10} \over {5 + 10}} + 10} \right]\,k\Omega $$

$$ = {{275} \over {15}}k\Omega $$

$$ \Rightarrow \Delta {V_{AB}} = 15\,mA \times {{275} \over {15}}k\Omega $$

$$ = 275$$ V

questionText: In the given figure, the value of Vo will be _____________ V.
subject: Physics

questionText: All resistances in figure are 1 $$\Omega$$ each. The value of current 'I' is $${a \over 5}$$ A. The value of a is _________.
subject: Physics
solution:
Let the current is i

Using Kirchhoff's law

$$iR + {i \over 2}R + {i \over 4}R + {i \over 8}R = 3$$

$$i = {{3 \times 8} \over {15}} = {8 \over 5}A$$

So $$a = 8$$

questionText: For the network shown below, the value of VB $$-$$ VA is ____________ V.
subject: Physics
solution:
$${V_B} - {V_A} = i \times 2$$

$$ = {{15} \over {1 + 2}} \times 2$$

$$ \Rightarrow {V_B} - {V_A} = 10$$ volts

The value of x to the nearest integer is ____________.

solution:

$$ \therefore $$ I = 3 mA

questionText: The value of current in the 6 $$\Omega$$ resistance is :
subject: Physics
solution:
The given figure can be represented as


From the above figure, it can be clearly seen that the voltage across point R is assumed as V.

Therefore, applying Kirchhoff's current law at point R, we can write

$${{V - 0} \over 6} + {{V - 90} \over 5} + {{V - 140} \over {20}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {V \over 6} + {{V - 90} \over 5} + {{V - 140} \over {20}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {{10V + 12V - 1080 + 3V - 420} \over {60}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow 25V = 1500 \Rightarrow V = 60V$$

Therefore, current through 6$$\Omega$$ resistor is

$$I = {V \over R} = {{60} \over 6} = 10A$$

questionText: In an electric circuit, a cell of certain emf provides a potential difference of 1.25 V across a load resistance of 5$$\Omega$$. However, it provides a potential difference of 1 V across a load resistance of 2$$\Omega$$. The emf of the cell is given by $${x \over {10}}V$$. Then the value of x is ______________.
subject: Physics
solution:
In case (a) $$\varepsilon = {{1.25} \over 5}(5 + r)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 4\varepsilon = 5 + r$$ ..... (1)

In case (b), $$\varepsilon = {1 \over 2}(2 + r)$$

$$ \Rightarrow 2\varepsilon = 2 + r$$ ..... (2)

From equation (1) & (2)

$$2\varepsilon = 3 \Rightarrow \varepsilon = 1.5$$

or x = 15

questionText: A current of 6A enters one corner P of an equilateral triangle PQR having 3 wires of resistance 2$$\Omega$$ each and leaves by the corner R. The currents i1 in ampere is _________.
subject: Physics
solution:
The current $${i_1} = \left( {{{{R_2}} \over {{R_1} + {R_2}}}} \right)i$$

$$ = \left( {{2 \over {4 + 2}}} \right) \times 6$$

$${i_1} = 2A$$

questionText: A 16 $$\Omega$$ wire is bend to form a square loop. A 9V supply having internal resistance of 1$$\Omega$$ is connected across one of its sides. The potential drop across the diagonals of the square loop is _______________ $$\times$$ 10<sup>$$-$$1</sup> V
subject: Physics
solution:
Here assume current as



By KVL in outer loop

9 $$-$$ 12i $$-$$ 4i = 0

16i = 9

8i = $${9 \over 2}$$ = 4.5

= 45 $$\times$$ 10-1

questionText:
Four resistances 40 $$\Omega $$, 60 $$\Omega $$, 90 $$\Omega $$ and 110 $$\Omega $$ make the arms of a quadrilateral ABCD. Across AC is
a battery of emf 40 V and internal resistance negligible.The potential difference across BD in V is
_______.
subject: Physics

questionText:
In the circuit, given in the figure currents in
different branches and value of one resistor are
shown. Then potential at point B with respect to
the point A is :
subject: Physics
solution:
Let us assume the potential at A = VA = 0

Now at junction C, According to KCL

i1 + i3 = i2

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 1 A + i3 = 2 A

$$ \Rightarrow $$ i3 = 1 A

Now Analyse potential along ACDB,

VA + 1 + i3(2) – 2 = VB

$$ \Rightarrow $$ 0 + 1 + 1(2) – 2 = VB

$$ \Rightarrow $$ VB = 3 – 2 = 1 V

questionText:
In the figure shown, the current in the 10 V battery is close to :
<picture><source media="(max-width: 320px)" srcset="https://imagex.cdn..net/jgy2xukgneaj1k/34a07c34-8ce6-420a-a29e-18e771ecebb8/35ed2680-15cf-11eb-8293-3b6cc591631c/file-jgy2xukgneaj1l-320w.jpg"><source media="(max-width: 500px)" srcset="https://imagex.cdn..net/jgy2xukgneaj1k/34a07c34-8ce6-420a-a29e-18e771ecebb8/35ed2680-15cf-11eb-8293-3b6cc591631c/file-jgy2xukgneaj1l-500w.jpg"></picture>
options: 0.36 A from negative to positive terminal
0.42 A from positive to negative terminal
0.71 A from positive to negative terminal
0.21 A from positive to negative terminal
subject: Physics
solution:
Apply Kirchhoff’s Current Law at junction A,

$${{x - \left( { - 20} \right)} \over 7} + {{x - \left( { - 10} \right)} \over 4} + {{x - 0} \over {10}}$$ = 0

$$ \Rightarrow $$ x = $$ - {{1500} \over {138}}$$ = -10.87 V

Current through 10 v,

i = $${{ - 10.87 - \left( { - 10} \right)} \over 4}$$ = - 0.21 A

As sign of current is negative so it will flow from positive to negative terminal.

questionText: The current I<sub>1</sub> (in A) flowing through 1 $$\Omega $$ resistor in the following circuit is :
subject: Physics
solution:
Equivalent resistance of upper branch of circuit R = 2.5 $$\Omega $$

Voltage across upper branch = 1 V

$$ \therefore $$ i1 = $${1 \over {2.5}}$$ = 0.4 A

i = $${{0.4} \over 2}$$ = 0.2 A

questionText:
In the given circuit diagram, a wire is joining
points B and D. The current in this wire is :
subject: Physics
solution:
I = $${{20} \over 2}$$ = 10 A

I1 = $${4 \over 5}I$$, I2 = $${1 \over 5}I$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ I1 = 8A, I2 = 2 A

I1' = $${3 \over 5} \times 10$$ = 6 A

I2' = $${2 \over 5} \times 10$$ = 4 A

$$ \Rightarrow $$ IBC = 8 – 6 = 2 A

questionText:
In the circuit shown, initially there is no charge on the capacitors and keys S1 and S2 are open. The values of the capacitors are C1 = 10$$\mu $$F, C2 = 30$$\mu $$F and C3 = C4 = 80$$\mu $$F.



Which of the statement(s) is/are correct?
options: The key S<sub>1</sub> is kept closed for long time such that capacitors are fully charged. Now, key S<sub>2</sub> is closed, at this time, the instantaneous current across 30$$\Omega $$ resistor (between points P and Q) will be 0.2 A (round off to 1<sup>st</sup> decimal place).
If key S<sub>1</sub> is kept closed for long time such that capacitors are fully charged, the voltage across the capacitor C<sub>1</sub> will be 4V.
At time t = 0, the key S<sub>1</sub> is closed, the instantaneous current in the closed circuit will be 25 mA.
If key S<sub>1</sub> is kept closed for long time such that the capacitors are fully charged, the voltage difference between points P and Q will be 10 V.
subject: Physics
solution:
Just after closing of switch charge on any capacitor is zero.

$$ \therefore $$ Replace all capacitors by conducting wires.



Current flow in the circuit,

$$i = {5 \over {70 + 100 + 30}} = {5 \over {200}} = 25$$ mA

Now S1 is kept closed for long time circuit is in steady state.



$${q \over {10}} + {q \over {80}} + {q \over {80}} - 5 = 0 \Rightarrow {{10q} \over {80}} = 5$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$q = 40\mu C$$

$$ \therefore $$ V across $${C_1} = {{40} \over {10}} = 4V$$

Now just after closing S2 charge on each capacitor remains same.



Applying KVL,

$$ - 10 + x \times 30 + {{40} \over {10}} + y \times 70 = 0$$

30x + 70y = 6 .....(1)

$$ - {{40} \over {80}} + 5 + (x - y)30 - {{40} \over {80}} + (x + y) \times 100 - 10 + x \times 30 = 0$$

$$160x - 130y - 6 = 0$$ .... (2)

$$y = {{96} \over {1510}}$$

x = 0.05 A

questionText:
In the figure shown, what is the current (in Ampere) drawn from the battery ?
You are given: R1 = 15$$\Omega $$, R2 = 10 $$\Omega $$, R3 = 20 $$\Omega $$, R4 = 5$$\Omega $$, R5 = 25$$\Omega $$, R6 = 30 $$\Omega $$, E = 15 V
subject: Physics
solution:
Equivalent resistance of the given circuit

$${R_{eq}} = 45 + {{10 \times 50} \over {10 + 50}} = \left( {45 + {{50} \over 6}} \right)\Omega $$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{160} \over 3}\Omega $$

$$ \therefore $$ $$I = {{15} \over {{{160} \over 3}}} = {9 \over {32}}A$$

questionText: In the given circuit the the internal resistance of the 18 V cell is negligible. If R1 = 400 $$\Omega $$, R3 = 100 $$\Omega $$ and R4 = 500 $$\Omega $$ and the reading of an ideal voltmeter across R4 is 5V, then the value of R2 will be :
subject: Physics
solution:
Voltage accross resistance R4 = 5 V

$$ \therefore $$   IR4 = 5 V

$$ \Rightarrow $$   500 $$ \times $$ I = 5

$$ \Rightarrow $$   I = $${1 \over {100}}$$ A

$$ \therefore $$   Voltage across resistor R3 = $${1 \over {100}}\left( {100} \right)$$ = 1 A

$$ \therefore $$   Total drop in resistance R3 and R4 = 5 + 1 = 6V

So, voltage accross R2 resistance is also 6V as R3, R4 and R2 are in parallel

$$ \therefore $$   Voltage accross R1 resistor R1 resistor = 18 $$-$$ 6 = 12 V

$$ \therefore $$   Current through R1 resistor = $${{12} \over {400}}$$ = $${3 \over {100}}$$ A

$$ \therefore $$   Current through R2 resistor

= $${3 \over {100}} - {1 \over {100}}$$

= $${2 \over {100}}$$ A

$$ \therefore $$   $$\left( {{2 \over {100}}} \right)$$ R2 = 6

$$ \Rightarrow $$   R2 = 300 $$\Omega $$

questionText: When the switch S, in the circuit shown, is closed, then the value of current i will be :
subject: Physics
solution:
We start by applying Kirchhoff's current law at point C in the circuit:

$$i_1+i_2=i$$

where $i_1$ and $i_2$ are the currents flowing through resistors R1 and R2 respectively, and i is the current flowing through the battery and resistor R3.

Next, we use Ohm's law to express $i_1$ and $i_2$ in terms of the voltages at points A, B, and C:

$$i_1=\frac{V_A-V_C}{R_1}=\frac{20-V_C}{2}$$

$$i_2=\frac{V_B-V_C}{R_2}=\frac{10-V_C}{4}$$

where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the resistances of resistors R1 and R2 respectively.

Substituting these expressions into the first equation, we get:

$$\frac{20-V_C}{2}+\frac{10-V_C}{4}=i$$

We then use Ohm's law again to express i in terms of the voltages at points C and earth :

$$i=\frac{V_C-V}{R_3}=\frac{V_C-0}{2}=\frac{V_C}{2}$$

Substituting this expression into the previous equation, we get:

$$\frac{20-V_C}{2}+\frac{10-V_C}{4}=\frac{V_C}{2}$$

Simplifying this equation, we get:

$$40-2V_C+10-V_C=2V_C$$

$$50=5V_C$$

$$V_C=10\text{ V}$$

Finally, we use Ohm's law to find the value of the current i:

$$i=\frac{V_C}{2}=\frac{10}{2}=5\text{ A}$$

Therefore, the current flowing through the circuit is 5 A.

questionText:
Two batteries with e.m.f 12 V and 13 V are connected in parallel across a load resistor of 10 $$\Omega $$. The
internal resistances of the two batteries are 1 $$\Omega $$ and 2 $$\Omega $$ respectively. The voltage across the load lies between :
subject: Physics
solution:
Let potential at S, T, R = V and potential at P, Q, U, = 0

Using kirchhoff's law at P :

Current at P is ,

$${{V - 12} \over 1} + {{V - 13} \over 2} + {{V - 0} \over {10}} = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$$$\,\,\,$$ $${V \over 1}$$ + $${V \over 2}$$ + $${V \over 10}$$ = 12 + $${{13} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$$$\,\,\,$$ $${{10V + 5V + V} \over {10}}$$ = $${{37} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$$$\,\,\,$$ $${{16V} \over {10}}$$ = $${{37} \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$$$\,\,\,$$ V = 11.56 Volt.

questionText: Which of the following statements is false?
options: Kirchhoff’s second law represents energy conservation.

Wheatstone bridge is the most sensitive when all the four resistances are of the same order of
magnitude.

In a balanced wheatstone bridge if the cell and the galvanometer are exchanged, the null point is
disturbed.
A rheostat can be used as a potential divider.
subject: Physics
solution:
There is no change in null point, if the cell and the
galvanometer are exchanged in a balanced wheatstone
bridge.

questionText: In the given circuit, the current in each resistance is:
subject: Physics
solution:
The potential difference in each loop is zero.

$$ \therefore $$ No current will flow or current in each resistance is Zero.

questionText: In the circuit shown below, the key is pressed at time t = 0. Which of the following statement(s) is (are) true?
options: The voltmeter display $$-$$5V as soon as the key is pressed and displays +5 V after a long time
The voltmeter will display 0 V at time t = ln 2 seconds
The current in the ammeter becomes 1/e of the initial value after 1 second
The current in the ammeter becomes zero after a long time
subject: Physics
solution:
When the key is just pressed, there is no charge on capacitors

$$\therefore$$ $$5 = 25000{I_1} \Rightarrow {I_1} = {5 \over {25000}} = 0.2\,mA$$

and $$5 = 50000{I_2} \Rightarrow {I_2} = {5 \over {50000}} = 0.1\,mA$$

Reading of voltmeter $$ = {V_Q} - {V_P} = - 25000{I_1}$$

$$ = - 25000 \times 0.2 \times {10^{ - 3}}V = - 5V$$

After a very long time, steady state is reached, i.e. $${I_1} - {I_2} = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$5 = {{{q_1}} \over {40 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}$$ or $${q_1} = 200\mu C$$

and $$\therefore$$ $$5 = {{{q_2}} \over {20 \times {{10}^{ - 6}}}}$$ or $${q_2} = 100\mu C$$

Now, reading of voltmeter $$ = {V_Q} - {V_P}$$

$$ = {{{q_2}} \over {20\mu F}} = {{100\mu C} \over {20\mu F}} = 5V$$

For capacitor of 40$$\mu$$F,

$${\tau _1} = RC = 25 \times {10^3} \times 40 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 1\,s$$

and for capacitor of 20$$\mu$$F,

$${\tau _2} = RC = 50 \times {10^3} \times 20 \times {10^{ - 6}} = 1\,s$$

$$\therefore$$ At any instant t,

$${q_1} = 200[1 - {e^{ - t}}]\mu C$$, $${q_2} = 100[1 - {e^{ - t}}]\mu C$$

$${I_1} = 0.2\,{e^{ - t}}$$ mA, $${I_2} = 0.1\,{e^{ - t}}$$ mA

$${V_Q} - {V_P} = {{100(1 - {e^{ - t}})\mu C} \over {20\mu F}} - 25k\Omega \times 0.2\,{e^{ - t}}$$ mA

$$ = 5(1 - {e^{ - t}}) - 5{e^{ - t}} = 5 - 10{e^{ - t}}$$

At t = ln 2 s; $${V_Q} - {V_P} = 5 - 10{e^{ - \ln 2}} = 5 - {{10} \over 2} = 0$$ V

Initially, I0 = 0.1 mA + 0.2 mA = 0.3 mA

At t = 1 s, I = I1 + I2

$$ = (0.2{e^{ - 1}} + 0.1{e^{ - 1}}) = {{0.3} \over e}\,mA = {{{I_0}} \over e}$$

After a long time, i.e. t $$\to$$ $$\infty$$

$$I = {I_1} + {I_2} = 0.2{e^{ - \infty }} + 0.1{e^{ - \infty }} = 0$$

questionText: In the following circuit, the current through the resistor R(=2$$\Omega$$) is I amperes. The value of I is :
subject: Physics
solution:
Consider the following figure:

ACGA constitutes a Wheatstone bridge; hence, 8 $$\Omega$$ is redundant and hence can be removed. Therefore,

$${R_{AG}} = {{3 \times 6} \over 9} = \,2\,\Omega $$

AGDFA again constitutes a Wheatstone bridge 10 $$\Omega$$ which is redundant and hence can be removed.

$${R_{AB}} = {{6 \times 18} \over {24}} = 4.5\,\,\Omega $$

$$I = {{6.5} \over {6.5}} = 1\,A$$

questionText: For the resistance network shown in the figure, choose the correct option(s).
options: The current through PQ is zero.
The potential at S is less than that at Q.
subject: Physics
solution:
Due to symmetry on upper side and lower side, points P and Q are at same potentials. Similarly, points S and T are at same potentials. Therefore, the simple circuit can be drawn as shown below :

$${I_2} = {{12} \over {2 + 2 + 2}} = 2A$$

$${I_3} = {{12} \over {4 + 4 + 4}} = 1A$$

$$\therefore$$ $${I_1} = {I_2} + {I_3} = 3A$$

IPQ = 0 because VP = VQ

Potential drop (from left to right) across each resistance is

$${{12} \over 3} = 4V$$

$$\therefore$$ $${V_{MS}} = 2 \times 4 = 8V$$

$${V_{NQ}} = 1 \times 4 = 4V$$

or, $${V_S} < {V_Q}$$

questionText: Two batteries of different emfs and different internal resistance are connected as shown. The voltage across AB in volts is __________.
subject: Physics
solution:
Applying Kirchhoff's second law for closed loop CDEFC we get

$$ - 3 - 2I - I + 6 = 0$$

$$I = {{6 - 3} \over 3} = 1A$$

For the lower path

$${V_A} - 3 - 2 \times 1 = {V_B}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${V_A} - {V_B} = 5V$$

We can also find the VAB by considering the upper path

For the upper path,

$${V_A} - 6 + 1 \times 1 = {V_B}$$

$${V_A} - {V_B} = 5V$$

questionText:
A $$5V$$ battery with internal resistance $$2\Omega $$ and a $$2V$$ battery with internal resistance $$1\Omega $$ are connected to a $$10\Omega $$ resistor as shown in the figure.

The current in the $$10\Omega $$ resistor is
subject: Physics
solution:
Applying kirchoff's loop law in $$AB\,{P_2}{P_1}A$$ we get

$$ - 2i + 5 - 10\,{i_1} = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( i \right)$$



Again applying kirchoffs loop law in $${P_2}$$ $$CD{P_1}{P_2}$$ we get,

$$10{i_1} + 2 - i + {i_1} = 0\,\,\,\,\,\,...\left( {ii} \right)$$

From $$\left( i \right)$$ and $$\left( {ii} \right)$$ $$11{i_1} + 2 - \left[ {{{5 - 10{i_1}} \over 2}} \right] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow {i_1} = {1 \over {32}}$$ A from $${P_2}$$ to $${P_1}$$

questionText: Two sources of equal $$emf$$ are connected to an external resistance  $$R.$$ The internal resistance of the two sources are $${R_1}$$ and  $${R_2}\left( {{R_1} > {R_1}} \right).$$ If the potential difference across the source having internal resistance $${R_2}$$ is zero, then
subject: Physics
solution:
$${\rm I} = {{2\varepsilon } \over {R + {R_1} + {R_2}}}$$

Potential difference across second cell

$$ = V = \varepsilon - {\rm I}{R_2} = 0$$

$$\varepsilon - {{2\varepsilon } \over {R + {R_1} + {R_2}}}.{R_2} = 0$$

$$R + {R_1} + {R_2} - 2{R_2} = 0$$

$$R + {R_1} - {R_2} = 0$$

$$\therefore$$ $$R = {R_2} - {R_1}$$

questionText: An electric current is passed through a circuit containing two wires of the same material, connected in parallel. If the lengths and radii are in the ratio of  $${4 \over 3}$$ and $${2 \over 3}$$, then the ratio of the current passing through the wires will be
subject: Physics
solution:
$${i_1}{R_1} = {i_2}{R_2}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ (same potential difference)

V = I1R1 = I1$$ \times $$$${{\rho {l_1}} \over {\pi r_1^2}}$$

Also V = I2R2 = I2$$ \times $$$${{\rho {l_2}} \over {\pi r_2^2}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ I1$$ \times $$$${{\rho {l_1}} \over {\pi r_1^2}}$$ = I2$$ \times $$$${{\rho {l_2}} \over {\pi r_2^2}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{{I_1}} \over {{I_2}}} = {{{\ell _1}} \over {{\ell _2}}} \times {{r_1^2} \over {r_2^2}}$$

$$ = {3 \over 4} \times {4 \over 9} = {1 \over 3}\,\,$$

questionText: The Kirchhoff's first law $$\left( {\sum i = 0} \right)$$ and second law $$\left( {\sum iR = \sum E} \right),$$ where the symbols have their usual meanings, are respectively based on
options: conservation  of charge, conservation of momentum
conservation of energy, conservation of charge
conservation of momentum, conservation of charge
conservation of charge, conservation of energy
subject: Physics
solution: NOTE : Kirchhoff's first law is based on conservation of charge and Kirchhoffs second law is based on conservation of energy.

questionText: A $$3$$ volt battery with negligible internal resistance is connected in a circuit as shown in the figure. The current $${\rm I}$$, in the circuit will be
subject: Physics
solution:

$$ \Rightarrow I = {V \over R} = {3 \over 2} = 1.5A$$
